question: [File: 'File Name: rest/service/nftService.js -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: getQuery()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the absence of any server-side query complexity limit in getQuery() to construct a query object where lower, inner, and upper each contain the maximum number of filter entries AND spenderIdInFilters contains the maximum number of entries, generating a SQL query of extreme complexity that causes the database query planner to spend disproportionate time on plan generation, increasing CPU consumption above 30%?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/nftService.js -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: getSubQuery()] Can an unprivileged external user supply a spenderIdInFilters array where each entry has a distinct value, causing the IN clause (line 72) to contain many unique values that cannot be optimized away by the database, forcing a per-row membership test against a large set in each of the three subqueries, increasing CPU consumption on the processing node above 30%?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/nftService.js -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: getQuery()] Can an unprivileged external user craft a query where the ownerAccountId corresponds to an account with a very large number of NFTs across many different token IDs, and then supply token_id range filters that span all those token IDs in the lower, inner, and upper arrays, causing each of the three subqueries to scan and sort a large result set before the outer LIMIT is applied, increasing processing node resource consumption above 30%?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/nftService.js -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: getNft()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the select * projection in nftByIdQuery (line 20: select * from nft where token_id = $1 and serial_number = $2) to retrieve all columns including potentially large metadata fields, and by issuing a moderate number of such requests targeting NFTs with large metadata, increase the total data transfer and serialization cost on the processing node above the 30% resource consumption threshold?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/nftService.js -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: getSubQuery()] Can an unprivileged external user supply both spenderIdFilters with many range conditions and spenderIdInFilters with many values simultaneously, causing getSubQuery() to generate a WHERE clause with many AND conditions plus a large IN clause, where this entire complex WHERE clause is replicated across all three subqueries in a single request, causing the database to evaluate a disproportionately expensive filter predicate three times per request?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/nftService.js -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: getQuery()] Can an unprivileged external user trigger the two-subquery UNION ALL path (when exactly two of lower, inner, upper are non-empty) with each subquery containing a large spenderIdInFilters list, causing two separate full-complexity queries to be executed and merged, increasing processing node resource consumption above 30% compared to a single simple query?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/nftService.js -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: getSubQuery()] Can an unprivileged external user craft filter values for the token_id column using boundary conditions (gt/lt) that create a very wide range, causing the subquery to scan a large portion of the nft table's composite index on (token_id, serial_number), returning a large intermediate result set that must be sorted by ORDER BY token_id, serial_number before LIMIT is applied, increasing I/O and sort memory usage on the processing node?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/nftService.js -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: getQuery()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that getQuery() does not validate or cap the number of entries in spenderIdInFilters before passing it to getSubQuery(), allowing an arbitrarily large IN clause to be generated and replicated across all three subqueries, causing unbounded growth in query complexity and processing node CPU consumption per request?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/nftService.js -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: getNfts()] Can an unprivileged external user invoke getNfts() with a query that generates a three-way UNION ALL where each subquery's nftQuery (lines 22-34) includes the coalesce(e.deleted, false) expression (line 26), causing the database to evaluate this expression for every row in every subquery's result set, tripling the per-row computation cost compared to a single subquery and increasing CPU consumption above 30%?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/nftService.js -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: getSubQuery()] Can an unprivileged external user supply filter conditions that create a serial_number range with no upper bound (e.g., serial_number > 0) combined with a token_id equality filter, causing the subquery to scan all serial numbers for a given token, and when this pattern is replicated across three subqueries for different token IDs, causing the database to perform three large index range scans per request?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/nftService.js -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: getQuery()] Can an unprivileged external user craft a query where the lower, inner, and upper arrays each contain filters for both token_id and serial_number with boundary operators, causing each of the three subqueries to require a composite index scan on (token_id, serial_number) with range conditions on both columns, which is more expensive than a single-column range scan, and when tripled via UNION ALL, increases processing node resource consumption above 30%?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/nftService.js -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: getSubQuery()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the absence of a maximum limit on the spenderIdFilters array length to supply many range conditions (e.g., spender >= 1, spender >= 2, ..., spender >= N) that are all appended to the conditions array via filters.push(...spenderIdFilters), creating a WHERE clause with N redundant range predicates that the database must evaluate per row, increasing per-row CPU cost above the 30% threshold?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/nftService.js -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: getQuery()] Can an unprivileged external user trigger the three-subquery UNION ALL path and simultaneously supply a large spenderIdInFilters list, causing the database to execute three separate complex queries and then merge, sort, and limit the combined result set, where the total work performed is proportional to 3 * limit * (cost of IN evaluation) + outer sort cost, exceeding the 30% resource consumption increase threshold with a single API request?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/nftService.js -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: getNft()] Can an unprivileged external user issue a moderate number of getNft() calls targeting NFTs that do not exist (returning null at line 40), causing the database to perform index lookups that return empty results, and if the nft table lacks an index on (token_id, serial_number), causing full table scans per call that cumulatively increase processing node resource consumption above 30%?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/nftService.js -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: getSubQuery()] Can an unprivileged external user craft a query where the conditions array in getSubQuery() (line 57) contains the accountIdCondition plus many filter conditions from both filters and spenderIdFilters, causing the resulting WHERE clause to have so many AND predicates that the database query planner's cost estimation becomes inaccurate, leading to a suboptimal query plan (e.g., sequential scan instead of index scan) that increases I/O and CPU consumption above 30%?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/nftService.js -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: getQuery()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the orderClause is computed once (line 89-91) and reused in all three subqueries and the outer query, where the ORDER BY token_id, serial_number clause forces the database to sort by two columns at every level, and by maximizing the number of rows returned by each subquery (via large limit), cause the total sort memory allocation to exceed normal levels and increase processing node resource consumption above 30%?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/nftService.js -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: getSubQuery()] Can an unprivileged external user supply a spenderIdInFilters array with values that span a wide range of spender IDs, combined with spenderIdFilters range conditions that overlap with the IN list, causing the database to evaluate both a range predicate and a large IN predicate per row in each subquery, where the combined filter evaluation cost per row is significantly higher than a simple equality check?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/nftService.js -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: getQuery()] Can an unprivileged external user craft a query where all three of lower, inner, and upper contain only a single filter entry each (minimally triggering the three-subquery path), but spenderIdInFilters contains the maximum number of entries, causing the large IN clause to be replicated three times in the UNION ALL query, increasing the per-request database CPU cost above 30% compared to a query without spenderIdInFilters?
